article.title: Astronomers Directly Confirm Two Protoplanets Forming Around a Young Sun-Like Star, Only the Second Such System Ever Observed
article.summary: The WISPIT 2 system, 437 light-years away, becomes only the second known case of two exoplanets caught in the act of forming, after PDS 70.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Astronomers have directly confirmed two gas giant protoplanets forming within the dusty disk around WISPIT 2, a five-million-year-old sun-like star located 437 light-years from Earth. The discovery, published in *The Astrophysical Journal Letters*, makes WISPIT 2 only the second known system — after PDS 70, first confirmed in 2018 — in which two exoplanets have been directly observed in the process of formation, according to [Scientific American](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/astronomers-witness-the-birth-of-a-new-solar-system/).

The findings were made possible by the upgraded GRAVITY+ instrument on the European Southern Observatory's Very Large Telescope Interferometer and offer an unprecedented window into the earliest stages of planetary system architecture.

## What We Know

The outer planet, WISPIT 2b, was first detected in 2025 using the VLT's SPHERE instrument. It has a mass of approximately 4.9 Jupiter masses and orbits at 57 astronomical units from its host star, according to [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-03-baby-planets-young-sun-star.html).

The newly confirmed inner planet, WISPIT 2c, is significantly more massive at 8 to 12 Jupiter masses and orbits four times closer to the star at 14 astronomical units, as reported by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-03-baby-planets-young-sun-star.html). The VLTI/GRAVITY+ instrument captured the planet's spectrum, revealing the presence of carbon dioxide — a common atmospheric component of gas giants that served as additional confirmation of its planetary nature.

WISPIT 2 itself is a pre-main-sequence star of approximately 1.08 solar masses that has not yet begun stable hydrogen fusion, according to [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-03-baby-planets-young-sun-star.html). At just five million years old, it represents a young analog of our own sun.

The star's protoplanetary disk displays a distinctive multi-ringed structure with multiple gaps carved by the forming planets as they accrete material through gravity. At least one additional, shallower gap exists farther out, potentially indicating a third planet of roughly Saturn mass. Lead author Chloe Lawlor stated that "these structures suggest that more planets are currently forming, which we will eventually detect," as quoted by [Scientific American](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/astronomers-witness-the-birth-of-a-new-solar-system/).

Lawlor, a Ph.D. student at the University of Galway's Centre for Astronomy and Ryan Institute, described the system as "the best look into our own past that we have to date," according to [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-03-baby-planets-young-sun-star.html).

Co-author Guillaume Bourdarot of the Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics emphasized the importance of the instrumentation: "The recent upgrade to GRAVITY+ enabled such clear detection of the planet so close to its star," as reported by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-03-baby-planets-young-sun-star.html).

## What We Don't Know

Whether the suspected third planet in the outer gap actually exists remains unconfirmed and will require further observations. The formation mechanisms that produce two massive gas giants at such different orbital distances within the same young disk are also not yet well understood.

While the orbital separations in both WISPIT 2 and PDS 70 may hint at optimal conditions for giant planet formation, researchers caution that the sample of directly observed forming planetary systems remains too small to draw firm conclusions. The researchers note their "results bring us one step closer to making direct connections between the initial conditions of planet formation and the final architectures of planetary systems," according to [Universe Today](https://www.universetoday.com/articles/direct-confirmation-of-two-baby-planets-forming-around-a-young-sun-like-star).

## Analysis

The significance of WISPIT 2 extends beyond the novelty of its second confirmed protoplanet. Co-author Christian Ginski of the University of Galway described the system as "a critical laboratory not just to observe the formation of a single planet but an entire planetary system," as reported by [Universe Today](https://www.universetoday.com/articles/direct-confirmation-of-two-baby-planets-forming-around-a-young-sun-like-star).

Compared to PDS 70, WISPIT 2 features more extended and resolved ring structures, which should provide superior opportunities for studying how multi-planet systems emerge, according to [Universe Today](https://www.universetoday.com/articles/direct-confirmation-of-two-baby-planets-forming-around-a-young-sun-like-star). The upcoming Extremely Large Telescope, with its 39-meter primary mirror, should enable detection of even fainter planets within the system, as noted by [Scientific American](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/astronomers-witness-the-birth-of-a-new-solar-system/).

The detection of carbon dioxide in WISPIT 2c's atmosphere demonstrates that atmospheric characterization is now possible for planets that are still actively forming — a capability that was science fiction a decade ago and that could transform understanding of how planetary atmospheres evolve from birth.
